Phylogenetics in R

Pol dataset

Louis du Plessis

Last modified: 22 Jun 2026

Pol dataset

First we read in the alignments.

pol <- read.dna(params$polfile, format = "fasta")

We can check to see that pol contains 42 sequences of length 689. We can also display the names of the sequences contained in each.

We also extract the names of the sequences that stem from the patient, the victim, and the community.

pol
## 42 DNA sequences in binary format stored in a matrix.
## 
## All sequences of same length: 689 
## 
## Labels:
## AY156771.1|LA02.RT 
## AY156793.1|LA30.RT 
## AY156783.1|LA18.RT 
## AY156778.1|LA12.RT 
## AY156792.1|LA29.RT 
## AY156773.1|LA05.RT 
## ...
## 
## Base composition:
##     a     c     g     t 
## 0.407 0.164 0.196 0.232 
## (Total: 28.94 kb)
labels(pol)
##  [1] "AY156771.1|LA02.RT "   "AY156793.1|LA30.RT "   "AY156783.1|LA18.RT "  
##  [4] "AY156778.1|LA12.RT "   "AY156792.1|LA29.RT "   "AY156773.1|LA05.RT "  
##  [7] "AY156795.1|LA32.RT "   "AY156775.1|LA07.RT "   "AY156774.1|LA06.RT "  
## [10] "AY156791.1|LA28.RT "   "AY156776.1|LA08.RT "   "AY156786.1|LA23.RT "  
## [13] "AY156789.1|LA26.RT "   "AY156785.1|LA22.RT "   "AY156788.1|LA25.RT "  
## [16] "AY156790.1|LA27.RT "   "AY156779.1|LA13.RT "   "AY156780.1|LA14.RT "  
## [19] "AY156781.1|LA16.RT "   "AY156782.1|LA17.RT "   "AY156799.1|P2.MIC.RT "
## [22] "AY156797.1|P1.MIC.RT " "AY156735.1|P2.BCM.RT " "AY156800.1|P3.MIC.RT "
## [25] "AY156802.1|P5.MIC.RT " "AY156737.1|P4.BCM.RT " "AY156740.1|P7.BCM.RT "
## [28] "AY156734.1|P1.BCM.RT " "AY156739.1|P6.BCM.RT " "AY156806.1|V1.MIC.RT "
## [31] "AY156807.1|V2.MIC.RT " "AY156803.1|P6.MIC.RT " "AY156738.1|P5.BCM.RT "
## [34] "AY156741.1|V1.BCM.RT " "AY156742.1|V2.BCM.RT " "AY156736.1|P3.BCM.RT "
## [37] "AY156801.1|P4.MIC.RT " "AY156784.1|LA21.RT "   "AY156787.1|LA24.RT "  
## [40] "AY156777.1|LA10.RT "   "AY156772.1|LA04.RT "   "AY156794.1|LA31.RT "
allpatients_pol <- sapply(strsplit(labels(pol), "\\|"), function(x) x[2])
patient_pol     <- labels(pol)[grep("^P", allpatients_pol)]
victim_pol      <- labels(pol)[grep("^V", allpatients_pol)]
community_pol   <- labels(pol)[grep("^LA", allpatients_pol)]

Distance-based trees

Calculate a neighbour-joining tree using a TN93 substitution model.

D_pol_TN93 <- ape::dist.dna(pol, model="TN93", as.matrix=TRUE)
tree_pol_NJ_TN93 <- phangorn::NJ(D_pol_TN93)

Check if the tree is rooted and if not, plot the unrooted tree.

if (!is.rooted(tree_pol_NJ_TN93)) {
    ape::plot.phylo(tree_pol_NJ_TN93, type = "unrooted", cex=0.5, lab4ut = "axial")
}

We assume the outgroup is AY156771.1|LA02.RT (one of the community sequences) and root the neighbour-joining tree on it, before plotting the rooted tree.

  tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted <- root.phylo(tree_pol_NJ_TN93,  
                                        outgroup = "AY156771.1|LA02.RT ", 
                                        resolve.root = TRUE)
  plot.phylo(tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted, align.tip.label = TRUE, cex=0.5)

Now create a colour array to colour patient, victim and community sequences differently using the tip.color argument in plot.phylo().

    colours <- rep("black", length(allpatients_pol))
    colours[match(patient_pol, tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted$tip.label)] <- 'blue'
    colours[match(victim_pol, tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted$tip.label)] <- 'red'
    
    plot.phylo(tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted, align.tip.label = TRUE, cex=0.5, 
               tip.color = colours)
    tiplabels(NA, pch=16, col=colours)
    legend("bottomleft", legend=c("Patient", "Victim", "Environment"),
           col=c("blue", "red", "black"), pch=16, cex=0.8)

Build the neighbour-joining tree using a Jukes-Cantor model, plot the tree and then plot the tanglegram connecting the Jukes Cantor NJ tree to the TN93 NJ tree.

# Construct and root Neighbour-joining tree
D_pol_JC <- ape::dist.dna(pol, model="JC", as.matrix=TRUE)
tree_pol_NJ_JC <- phangorn::NJ(D_pol_JC)
tree_pol_NJ_JC_rooted <- root.phylo(tree_pol_NJ_JC,  
                                    outgroup = "AY156771.1|LA02.RT ", 
                                    resolve.root = TRUE)

# Plot the tree
plot.phylo(tree_pol_NJ_JC_rooted, align.tip.label = TRUE, cex=0.5, 
           tip.color = colours)
legend("bottomleft", legend=c("Patient", "Victim", "Environment"),
           col=c("blue", "red", "black"), pch=16, cex=0.8)

# Plot the tanglegram
cophyloplot(tree_pol_NJ_JC_rooted, tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted, assoc=cbind(tree_pol_NJ_JC_rooted$tip.label, tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted$tip.label), show.tip.label = FALSE, space=100, gap =5, length.line=0.2)

Parsimony

Calculate the parsimony score of the NJ tree. To use the function we have to first convert the alignment to an object of type phyDat, which is used by the phangorn package.

  pol_phyDat <- as.phyDat(pol)  
  parsimony(tree_pol_NJ_JC, pol_phyDat)
## [1] 398

Now use the pratchet() function to find the maximum parsimony tree and then use acctran() to adjust the branch lengths, and again plot the tree.

  # Calculate the maximum parsimony tree
  tree_pol_MP <- pratchet(pol_phyDat)
## Parsimony score of initial tree: 369 
## Iteration: 10. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 20. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 30. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 40. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 50. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 60. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 70. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 80. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 90. Best parsimony score so far: 366Iteration: 100. Best parsimony score so far: 366
  tree_pol_MP <- acctran(tree_pol_MP, data = pol_phyDat)
  
  # Plot it
  colours <- rep("black", length(tree_pol_MP$tip.label))
  colours[match(patient_pol, tree_pol_MP$tip.label)] <- 'blue'
  colours[match(victim_pol, tree_pol_MP$tip.label)] <- 'red'
    
  plot.phylo(tree_pol_MP, align.tip.label = TRUE, cex=0.7, 
               tip.color = colours)
  tiplabels(NA, pch=16, col=colours)
  legend("bottomleft", legend=c("Patient", "Victim", "Environment"),
       col=c("blue", "red", "black"), pch=16, cex=0.8)

We can also verify that the maximum parsimony tree has a lower parsimony score than the NJ tree.

  parsimony(tree_pol_NJ_TN93, pol_phyDat)
## [1] 398
  parsimony(tree_pol_MP, pol_phyDat)
## [1] 366
  parsimony(tree_pol_NJ_TN93, pol_phyDat) < parsimony(tree_pol_MP, pol_phyDat)
## [1] FALSE

Now we plot the tanglegram comparing the neighbour-joining tree to the maximum parsimony tree.

  # Root on the same sequence
  tree_pol_MP_rooted <- root.phylo(tree_pol_MP,  
                                   outgroup = "AY156771.1|LA02.RT ", 
                                   resolve.root = TRUE)
  
  # Plot the tanglegram
  cophyloplot(tree_pol_MP_rooted, tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted,
              assoc=cbind(tree_pol_MP_rooted$tip.label, 
                          tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted$tip.label), 
              show.tip.label = FALSE, space=100, gap =5, length.line=0.1)

Maximum-likelihood

Calculate the maximum-likelihood tree using the GTR model. Since the tree is unrooted (check it!) also root it on the same outgroup.

  tree_pol_ML_GTR <- pml_bb(pol, model="GTR")
## optimize edge weights:  -3313.037 --> -3309.2 
## optimize rate matrix:  -3309.2 --> -3144.562 
## optimize edge weights:  -3144.562 --> -3143.708 
## optimize topology:  -3143.708 --> -3137.369  NNI moves:  7 
## optimize rate matrix:  -3137.369 --> -3137.313 
## optimize edge weights:  -3137.313 --> -3137.312 
## optimize topology:  -3137.312 --> -3137.312  NNI moves:  0 
## optimize topology:  -3292.671 --> -3138.99  NNI moves:  17 
## Ratchet iteration  1 , best pscore so far: -3137.312 
## optimize topology:  -3206.364 --> -3143.535  NNI moves:  13 
## Ratchet iteration  2 , best pscore so far: -3137.312 
## optimize topology:  -3249.801 --> -3139.546  NNI moves:  21 
## Ratchet iteration  3 , best pscore so far: -3137.312 
## optimize topology:  -3216.215 --> -3130.25  NNI moves:  16

## optimize rate matrix:  -3127.841 --> -3127.841 
## optimize edge weights:  -3127.841 --> -3127.841
  tree_pol_ML_GTR_rooted <- root.phylo(tree_pol_ML_GTR$tree,  
                                       outgroup = "AY156771.1|LA02.RT ", 
                                       resolve.root = TRUE)

Now plot the ML tree.

  colours <- rep("black", length(tree_pol_ML_GTR_rooted$tip.label))
  colours[match(patient_pol, tree_pol_ML_GTR_rooted$tip.label)] <- 'red'
  colours[match(victim_pol, tree_pol_ML_GTR_rooted$tip.label)] <- 'blue'
    
  plot.phylo(tree_pol_ML_GTR_rooted, align.tip.label = TRUE, cex=0.7, 
               tip.color = colours)
  tiplabels(NA, pch=16, col=colours)
  legend("bottomleft", legend=c("Patient", "Victim", "Environment"),
       col=c("blue", "red", "black"), pch=16, cex=0.8)

Tanglegram between the NJ and ML trees

  # Plot the tanglegram
  cophyloplot(tree_pol_ML_GTR_rooted, tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted,
              assoc=cbind(tree_pol_ML_GTR_rooted$tip.label, 
                          tree_pol_NJ_TN93_rooted$tip.label), 
              show.tip.label = FALSE, space=100, gap =5, length.line=0.1)
